content.text:
To opt out of anonymous install/update telemetry tied to changelog detection, set `enableInstallTelemetry` to `false` in `settings.json`, or set `PI_TELEMETRY=0`.

---

## Context Files

Pi loads `AGENTS.md` (or `CLAUDE.md`) at startup from:
- `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md` (global)
- Parent directories (walking up from cwd)
- Current directory

Use for project instructions, conventions, common commands. All matching files are concatenated.

Disable context file loading with `--no-context-files` (or `-nc`).

### System Prompt

Replace the default system prompt with `.pi/SYSTEM.md` (project) or `~/.pi/agent/SYSTEM.md` (global). Append without replacing via `APPEND_SYSTEM.md`.

---

## Customization

### Prompt Templates

Reusable prompts as Markdown files. Type `/name` to expand.

```markdown
<!-- ~/.pi/agent/prompts/review.md -->
Review this code for bugs, security issues, and performance problems.
Focus on: {{focus}}
```

Place in `~/.pi/agent/prompts/`, `.pi/prompts/`, or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/prompt-templates.md](docs/prompt-templates.md).

### Skills

On-demand capability packages following the [Agent Skills standard](https://agentskills.io). Invoke via `/skill:name` or let the agent load them automatically.

```markdown

content.text:
> pi can create prompt templates. Ask it to build one for your workflow.

# Prompt Templates

Prompt templates are Markdown snippets that expand into full prompts. Type `/name` in the editor to invoke a template, where `name` is the filename without `.md`.

## Locations

Pi loads prompt templates from:

- Global: `~/.pi/agent/prompts/*.md`
- Project: `.pi/prompts/*.md`
- Packages: `prompts/` directories or `pi.prompts` entries in `package.json`
- Settings: `prompts` array with files or directories
- CLI: `--prompt-template <path>` (repeatable)

Disable discovery with `--no-prompt-templates`.

## Format

```markdown
---
description: Review staged git changes
---
Review the staged changes (`git diff --cached`). Focus on:
- Bugs and logic errors
- Security issues
- Error handling gaps
```

- The filename becomes the command name. `review.md` becomes `/review`.
- `description` is optional. If missing, the first non-empty line is used.
- `argument-hint` is optional. When set, the hint is displayed before the description in the autocomplete dropdown.

### Argument Hints

Use `argument-hint` in frontmatter to show expected arguments in autocomplete. Use `<angle brackets>` for required arguments and `[square brackets]` for optional ones:

```markdown
---
description: Review PRs from URLs with structured issue and code analysis
argument-hint: "<PR-URL>"
---
```

This renders in the autocomplete dropdown as:

```
→ pr   <PR-URL>       — Review PRs from URLs with structured issue and code analysis
  is   <issue>        — Analyze GitHub issues (bugs or feature requests)
  wr   [instructions] — Finish the current task end-to-end
  cl   — Audit changelog entries before release
```

## Usage

Type `/` followed by the template name in the editor. Autocomplete shows available templates with descriptions.

```
/review                           # Expands review.md
/component Button                 # Expands with argument
/component Button "click handler" # Multiple arguments
```

## Arguments

Templates support positional arguments and simple slicing:

- `$1`, `$2`, ... positional args
- `$@` or `$ARGUMENTS` for all args joined
- `${@:N}` for args from the Nth position (1-indexed)
- `${@:N:L}` for `L` args starting at N

Example:

```markdown
---
description: Create a component
---
Create a React component named $1 with features: $@

| `qna.ts` | Q&A with custom UI | `registerCommand`, `ui.custom`, `setEditorText` |
| `send-user-message.ts` | Inject user messages | `registerCommand`, `sendUserMessage` |
| `reload-runtime.ts` | Reload command and LLM tool handoff | `registerCommand`, `ctx.reload()`, `sendUserMessage` |
| `shutdown-command.ts` | Graceful shutdown command | `registerCommand`, `shutdown()` |
| **Events & Gates** |||
| `permission-gate.ts` | Block dangerous commands | `on("tool_call")`, `ui.confirm` |
| `protected-paths.ts` | Block writes to specific paths | `on("tool_call")` |
| `confirm-destructive.ts` | Confirm session changes | `on("session_before_switch")`, `on("session_before_fork")` |
| `dirty-repo-guard.ts` | Warn on dirty git repo | `on("session_before_*")`, `exec` |
| `input-transform.ts` | Transform user input | `on("input")` |
| `model-status.ts` | React to model changes | `on("model_select")`, `setStatus` |
| `provider-payload.ts` | Inspect payloads and provider response headers | `on("before_provider_request")`, `on("after_provider_response")` |
| `system-prompt-header.ts` | Display system prompt info | `on("agent_start")`, `getSystemPrompt` |
| `claude-rules.ts` | Load rules from files | `on("session_start")`, `on("before_agent_start")` |
| `file-trigger.ts` | File watcher triggers messages | `sendMessage` |
| **Compaction & Sessions** |||
| `custom-compaction.ts` | Custom compaction summary | `on("session_before_compact")` |
| `trigger-compact.ts` | Trigger compaction manually | `compact()` |
| `git-checkpoint.ts` | Git stash on turns | `on("turn_start")`, `on("session_before_fork")`, `exec` |
| `auto-commit-on-exit.ts` | Commit on shutdown | `on("session_shutdown")`, `exec` |
| **UI Components** |||
| `status-line.ts` | Footer status indicator | `setStatus`, session events |
| `custom-footer.ts` | Replace footer entirely | `registerCommand`, `setFooter` |
| `custom-header.ts` | Replace startup header | `on("session_start")`, `setHeader` |
| `modal-editor.ts` | Vim-style modal editor | `setEditorComponent`, `CustomEditor` |
| `rainbow-editor.ts` | Custom editor styling | `setEditorComponent` |
| `widget-placement.ts` | Widget above/below editor | `setWidget` |
| `overlay-test.ts` | Overlay components | `ui.custom` with overlay options |
| `overlay-qa-tests.ts` | Comprehensive overlay tests | `ui.custom`, all overlay options |
| `notify.ts` | Simple notifications | `ui.notify` |
| `timed-confirm.ts` | Dialogs with timeout | `ui.confirm` with timeout/signal |
| `mac-system-theme.ts` | Auto-switch theme | `setTheme`, `exec` |
| **Complex Extensions** |||

content.thinking:
**Organizing AGENTS resources**

I’m thinking about how to manage resources for different subprojects. It might be helpful to have a nested `AGENTS.md` file in those directories. Using symlinks with stable names could simplify things, so AGENTS could reference stable names instead of full paths, which could change. I should consider maintaining a machine-readable file like `.agent/paths.md` to avoid clutter as well. I wonder if Pi can automatically read arbitrary linked files — I’ll need to verify that too.

**Considering AGENTS context**

I’m thinking about how to handle skills for on-demand context instead of always-on. If there are many paths or if they're verbose, a skill could direct where to find them. The user is asking about adding paths to the agent's context. For static paths, AGENTS can directly reference them, but frequently changing paths might require a custom extension or prompt template. I should also note that the settings for `prompts` allow for local prompt template directories, though maybe it’s not essential to mention that.

**Considering path integration**

I'm thinking about a more ergonomic way to handle prompts using the `/paths` template. Also, for a robust approach, I could create an extension that reads from `.pi/resources.json` and appends to the system prompt each session. Since the user is asking how to simplify adding those paths to the agent's context, it seems like they’re looking for an efficient mechanism to streamline the process. Let's find the best solution together!

**Exploring build directory stability**

I'm considering how to maintain stability in the repo root. For changing build directories, using stable symlink names or a script seems effective. For instance, keeping the `out/current` symlink pointing to the active build directory and including `build dir: out/current` in AGENTS could prevent frequent AGENTS updates when the build directory changes. This is a solid concept, and I could also suggest using a similar `spec/current` symlink as a robust pattern. Overall, symlinks or aliases are a strong choice due to changing paths.

**Evaluating path info loading**

I’m thinking about how path info loads only when working within a specific subtree. It seems that Pi loads parent directories from the current working directory (cwd). If the cwd is the root, nested paths aren’t loaded. But when I navigate into `ets2panda/`, it includes that too. This could be useful for working under specific subsystems. However, there’s a potential issue if the user changes the cwd during the session, maybe needing a reload after changing directories. I should mention this.

**Crafting a useful extension**

It could be helpful to mention that I can write this extension for the user. Since they’re asking in general terms, it might be good to offer this option. I could say something like, “If you want, I can set up AGENTS.md, a /paths prompt, and a small extension in this repo.” I need to ensure my answer remains concise and not overly verbose, aiming for around 8-12 bullet points to keep it clear and helpful.

content.text:
The simplest native way in pi is to use **`AGENTS.md` as a single source of truth** for canonical resource paths.

### 1) Put the paths in `AGENTS.md`
Pi auto-loads:

- `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md` for global context
- `AGENTS.md` files from parent dirs up to the current dir

So you can keep a short section like:

```md
## Canonical resource paths
- Language spec: `docs/spec/current`
- Main tests: `ets2panda/test`
- Active build dir: `out/current`
- SDK tests: `test/`
```

When a path changes, update **one file** and run:

```text
/reload
```

---

### 2) Prefer stable aliases over real changing paths
If paths change often, don’t put the volatile path directly into agent context.

Instead, create stable repo-local aliases, for example:

- `out/current` → symlink to the current build dir
- `docs/spec/current` → symlink to the current spec
- `test/current-suite` → symlink to the active tests

Then `AGENTS.md` never changes much; only the symlink target does.

This is usually the cleanest option.

---

### 3) Use repo-relative paths, not absolute ones
Prefer:

```md
- Build dir: `out/current`
```

over:

```md
- Build dir: `/home/me/work/project/out/linux_clang_release_123`
```

Relative paths are shorter, more portable, and easier for the agent to use.

---

### 4) If you only need them sometimes, create a prompt template
If you don’t want these paths always in context, add a prompt template like:

`~/.pi/agent/prompts/paths.md` or `.pi/prompts/paths.md`

```md
---
description: Insert canonical project paths
---
Use these canonical paths:
- Language spec: `docs/spec/current`
- Tests: `ets2panda/test`
- Build dir: `out/current`
```

Then just type:

```text
/paths
```

---

### 5) For dynamic paths, use a tiny config file + extension
If some paths are computed or change frequently, the best long-term setup is:

- keep them in a file like `.pi/resources.json`
- use a small pi extension to inject them into context on session start

That gives you one editable config instead of rewriting prompts manually.

Example config:

```json
{
  "languageSpec": "docs/spec/current",
  "tests": "ets2panda/test",
  "buildDir": "out/current"
}
```

Then the extension reads it and appends it to the system prompt automatically.

---

## Recommended setup
For most projects, I’d use this:

1. **`AGENTS.md`** with a short “Canonical resource paths” section
2. **stable symlinks** like `out/current`, `docs/spec/current`
3. optional **`/paths` prompt template** for on-demand insertion

That keeps the agent context simple and avoids repeating full paths in chat.

If you want, I can help you set up:
- a good `AGENTS.md` section,
- a `.pi/prompts/paths.md` template,
- or a tiny extension that loads paths from a config file.

**Considering security and extensions**

I’m thinking about potential security issues with secret environment variables. It’s possible that the environment isn’t included in the system prompt unless it’s specifically appended. I might need to mention that some mechanism inside the PI will read those environment variables — could be via an extension command or system prompt. I should also look into modifying the system prompt before the agent starts and verify the locations for local extension loading. Time to check the documentation!

**Considering shell environment and tools**

I’m thinking about the `bash` tool and shell environment. Since `pi` starts from the shell, the built-in `bash` tool can access that environment. It could execute commands like `echo $PI_BUILD_DIR` if given the instruction. However, there are concerns about relying on it, as the model might forget variable names. It could be simpler to document environment variable names in an AGENTS.md file and use a wrapper script to set those values. But the model would still need a way to read them via `bash`.

**Examining environment variable usage**

I'm considering how to handle environment variables in the AGENTS context, specifically `PI_LANG_SPEC`, `PI_TEST_DIR`, and `PI_BUILD_DIR`. Instead of injecting paths every time, it might be better for the agent to access these variables using bash commands like `printf '%s\n' "$PI_BUILD_DIR"`. The user wants a way for pi to read environment variables directly, and while there's no built-in generic mechanism for this, documenting variable names in AGENTS.md would let the agent fetch them as needed. Additionally, I want to verify if the bash tool inherits the environment correctly.

**Considering shell inheritance in examples**

I'm thinking about supporting my explanation with an example from the bash-spawn-hook extension, but I’m not sure if it’s really necessary. However, I wonder if I should hold back from leaning too heavily on that without solid documentation to back it up. It’s important to keep my information clear and accurate for the user!

**Exploring bash tool usage**

I'm thinking about the built-in bash tool and how it likely does what the user needs. Since they asked about pi, I want to make sure I'm accurate, maybe saying something like "in practice" instead. I feel it's important to clarify that there's a hook for adding environment variables, but the built-in tool inherits its environment from pi already. For live updates without a restart, using an extension that reads a file, like `.pi/paths.json`, with each prompt is a good option.

content.thinking:
**Exploring bash tool options**

It’s interesting that the bash tool supports a spawn hook for adjusting the environment, but that's for a custom bash tool, not the built-in one. If the user wants environment variables available in bash, even if they're not set on the pi process, they could create a custom bash tool or override it. However, the main question is about the agent context. A direct command like `--append-system-prompt "$(printf...)"` could be simpler for the user. I think I should suggest a global wrapper script along with project-local agents.

**Examining environment variable handling**

The agent can read live values through bash, and the environment can be modified within the pi process, but not from the parent shell. Using `source config.env` for each bash command isn't automatic. If pi's bash tool inherits environment variables that are fixed at startup, it should work with a wrapper across sessions. I should probably mention that using environment variables could be less preferable than files because they can be less visible or auditable, don't show in startup headers, and require restarts for changes.

**Suggesting a file-based pattern**

I think a good approach would be to have a script generate a small Markdown or JSON file for paths instead of using environment variables. This way, the extension or AGENTS can reference that file, making it clearer since environment variables can be quite opaque. If someone asks about writing a script, I'd recommend favoring a file-based solution. It's important to note that pi can only automatically discover AGENTS, prompts, and skills, not a generic environment loader. Extensions can read `process.env` since they're running in Node, which is a nice feature.

**Exploring session values**

I’m considering if I can insert the environment values just once per session using `session_start` along with `ctx.appendCustomMessageEntry`. I’ll need to search for session append functions to confirm. It sounds like I could even create a persistent message manually, but I might need the documentation for that. Using `before_agent_start` each turn seems simpler, though I worry about token duplication. Maybe I can mention "persistent context with custom message" when using an extension!

**Considering persistent messaging**

I’m checking if a persistent message can be injected as stated in the docs. It looks like it can be stored for context once, but if values change, it might become stale, requiring update logic. Editing a previous message could be tricky. Using the `--append-system-prompt <text>` option seems beneficial for maintaining context across sessions, as it stays static for the entire session. A wrapper script can manage this at startup without needing an extension.

**Exploring environment variable integration**

If values change during the same running session, I might need to restart or consider using an extension that reads from a file. It’s worth noting the `APPEND_SYSTEM.md` file, as a script could generate this. I’m curious if Pi reloads context files and these append files when using `/reload`, which refreshes various resources. It seems like it might work, but it's unclear. In terms of user scripts interacting with environment variables, the answer is yes, but extensions or CLI appending may be necessary for this.

**Clarifying environment variable use**

I’m thinking that I probably wouldn’t recommend using environment variables unless the values are specific to each shell or session. For configurations that change frequently, it seems like using a file might be a better option. This way, it’s clearer and easier to manage, especially if the project settings need to be updated often. Let’s put together a concise and helpful response that explains this approach effectively.

bash tool options**

It’s interesting that the bash tool supports a spawn hook for adjusting the environment, but that's for a custom bash tool, not the built-in one. If the user wants environment variables available in bash, even if they're not set on the pi process, they could create a custom bash tool or override it. However, the main question is about the agent context. A direct command like `--append-system-prompt "$(printf...)"` could be simpler for the user. I think I should suggest a global wrapper script along with project-local agents."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Examining environment variable handling**

The agent can read live values through bash, and the environment can be modified within the pi process, but not from the parent shell. Using `source config.env` for each bash command isn't automatic. If pi's bash tool inherits environment variables that are fixed at startup, it should work with a wrapper across sessions. I should probably mention that using environment variables could be less preferable than files because they can be less visible or auditable, don't show in startup headers, and require restarts for changes."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Suggesting a file-based pattern**

I think a good approach would be to have a script generate a small Markdown or JSON file for paths instead of using environment variables. This way, the extension or AGENTS can reference that file, making it clearer since environment variables can be quite opaque. If someone asks about writing a script, I'd recommend favoring a file-based solution. It's important to note that pi can only automatically discover AGENTS, prompts, and skills, not a generic environment loader. Extensions can read `process.env` since they're running in Node, which is a nice feature."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Exploring session values**

I’m considering if I can insert the environment values just once per session using `session_start` along with `ctx.appendCustomMessageEntry`. I’ll need to search for session append functions to confirm. It sounds like I could even create a persistent message manually, but I might need the documentation for that. Using `before_agent_start` each turn seems simpler, though I worry about token duplication. Maybe I can mention "persistent context with custom message" when using an extension!"},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Considering persistent messaging**

I’m checking if a persistent message can be injected as stated in the docs. It looks like it can be stored for context once, but if values change, it might become stale, requiring update logic. Editing a previous message could be tricky. Using the `--append-system-prompt <text>` option seems beneficial for maintaining context across sessions, as it stays static for the entire session. A wrapper script can manage this at startup without needing an extension."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Exploring environment variable integration**

If values change during the same running session, I might need to restart or consider using an extension that reads from a file. It’s worth noting the `APPEND_SYSTEM.md` file, as a script could generate this. I’m curious if Pi reloads context files and these append files when using `/reload`, which refreshes various resources. It seems like it might work, but it's unclear. In terms of user scripts interacting with environment variables, the answer is yes, but extensions or CLI appending may be necessary for this."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Clarifying environment variable use**

I’m thinking that I probably wouldn’t recommend using environment variables unless the values are specific to each shell or session. For configurations that change frequently, it seems like using a file might be a better option. This way, it’s clearer and easier to manage, especially if the project settings need to be updated often. Let’s put together a concise and helpful response that explains this approach effectively."}]}

content.text:
Yes — **that can work well**, but with one caveat:

## Important caveat
Pi does **not** have a built-in generic feature like:

> “take arbitrary env vars and automatically put them into model context”

So if you export:

```bash
export PI_LANG_SPEC=...
export PI_TEST_DIR=...
export PI_BUILD_DIR=...
```

pi itself won’t automatically show those to the agent unless you add a mechanism.

---

## Good ways to use env vars with pi

### Option 1: Wrapper script + `--append-system-prompt`
This is the simplest.

Your launcher script computes paths, exports them, and starts pi with an appended system prompt:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
export PI_LANG_SPEC="docs/spec/current"
export PI_TEST_DIR="ets2panda/test"
export PI_BUILD_DIR="out/current"

exec pi \
  --append-system-prompt "$(cat <<EOF
Canonical project paths:
- Language spec: \`$PI_LANG_SPEC\`
- Test dir: \`$PI_TEST_DIR\`
- Build dir: \`$PI_BUILD_DIR\`
Prefer these when relevant.
EOF
)" \
  "$@"
```

### Pros
- no extension needed
- easy to maintain
- values are visible to the agent immediately

### Cons
- values are fixed when pi starts
- if env changes, you need to restart pi

---

### Option 2: Small pi extension reading `process.env`
This is the most “pi-native” way.

Put this in:

```text
.pi/extensions/paths-from-env.ts
```

```ts
import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent";

const PATH_VARS: Array<[string, string]> = [
  ["Language spec", "PI_LANG_SPEC"],
  ["Test dir", "PI_TEST_DIR"],
  ["Build dir", "PI_BUILD_DIR"],
];

export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  pi.on("before_agent_start", async (event) => {
    const lines = PATH_VARS
      .map(([label, envName]) => {
        const value = process.env[envName];
        return value ? `- ${label}: \`${value}\`` : null;
      })
      .filter(Boolean)
      .join("\n");

    if (!lines) {
      return;
    }

    return {
      systemPrompt:
        event.systemPrompt +
        `

## Canonical project paths

${lines}

Prefer these paths when relevant.
`,
    };
  });
}
```

Then export env vars before starting pi:

```bash
export PI_LANG_SPEC=docs/spec/current
export PI_TEST_DIR=ets2panda/test
export PI_BUILD_DIR=out/current
pi
```

### Pros
- clean separation
- reusable across projects
- easy to extend

### Cons
- still tied to pi process environment
- if env changes after pi starts, running pi usually won’t see updated shell env; restart pi

---

### Option 3: Keep env vars hidden, let agent fetch them via `bash`
If you want to save context tokens, you can just document the **variable names** in `AGENTS.md`:

```md
## Canonical resource env vars
- `PI_LANG_SPEC`
- `PI_TEST_DIR`
- `PI_BUILD_DIR`

When you need them, use bash to print them.
```

Then your launcher exports the values.

The agent can do:

```bash
printf '%s\n' "$PI_BUILD_DIR"
```

### Pros
- minimal context overhead
- paths stay dynamic within bash calls

### Cons
- extra tool call needed
- agent must remember to query them

---

## My recommendation

### If paths only change between sessions
Use:

- **wrapper script**
- plus either:
  - `--append-system-prompt`, or
  - a tiny extension

### If paths may change while pi is already running
**Env vars are not the best choice.**  
Use a file instead, e.g.:

```text
.pi/paths.json
```

or

```text
.pi/APPEND_SYSTEM.md
```

Then either:
- have your script rewrite that file
- use `/reload`
- or make extension reread the file on every prompt

That is more reliable than env, because parent-shell env changes do not magically propagate into an already running pi process.

---

## Best practical pattern
I’d rank them like this:

1. **Generated file** (`.pi/APPEND_SYSTEM.md` or `.pi/paths.json`) → best for changing values
2. **Wrapper + `--append-system-prompt`** → best for simple startup-only values
3. **Extension reading `process.env`** → best if you want a reusable pi integration
4. **Env vars only + bash lookup** → lowest token cost

---

## One more warning
If you inject env values into system prompt/context, they are sent to the model.  
So only expose **safe path variables**, not general env.

---

If you want, I can write one of these for you:

1. a `run-pi.sh` wrapper,  
2. `.pi/extensions/paths-from-env.ts`, or  
3. a `.pi/paths.json` + extension setup.

content.text:
Option 4 was:

## Env vars only + bash lookup

Meaning:

- your script sets the paths as environment variables
- **pi does not inject them into model context automatically**
- instead, the agent learns only the **variable names**
- when it needs an actual path, it uses the **`bash` tool** to read them

### Example

Start pi via a wrapper:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
export PI_LANG_SPEC="docs/spec/current"
export PI_TEST_DIR="ets2panda/test"
export PI_BUILD_DIR="out/current"

exec pi "$@"
```

And put this in `AGENTS.md`:

```md
## Canonical resource env vars
- `PI_LANG_SPEC` — language spec path
- `PI_TEST_DIR` — main test directory
- `PI_BUILD_DIR` — current build directory

If you need one of these paths, use bash to print it.
Example:
`printf '%s\n' "$PI_LANG_SPEC"`
```

Then the agent can run:

```bash
printf '%s\n' "$PI_BUILD_DIR"
```

or:

```bash
env | rg '^PI_(LANG_SPEC|TEST_DIR|BUILD_DIR)='
```

---

## Why use it

### Pros
- no permanent context/token cost for the actual path values
- paths can be centralized in one wrapper script
- simple setup, no extension needed

### Cons
- extra `bash` call is needed before using a path
- the agent won’t see the values unless it asks for them
- env values are effectively fixed for that pi process after startup

---

## When it’s good
Use this if:

- you want to keep context small
- paths are needed only sometimes
- you’re okay with the agent doing one lookup command

## When it’s not ideal
Don’t use it if:

- you want the agent to proactively know the paths every turn
- you want path changes to be reflected in a running pi session without restart

In those cases, generated file / extension is better.

If you want, I can show a **minimal real setup** for option 4 for your project.

content.text:
Yes — then **don’t put this into the project’s `AGENTS.md`**.

Use a **personal layer** instead.

## Best choices without touching shared repo files

### 1. Global personal `AGENTS.md`
Put your private instructions in:

```text
~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md
```

Example:

```md
## Personal repo-specific hints

Only for repo `/home/mbolshov/front_work`:

Canonical resource env vars may be available:
- `PI_LANG_SPEC`
- `PI_TEST_DIR`
- `PI_BUILD_DIR`

If needed, query them with bash, e.g.:
`printf '%s\n' "$PI_BUILD_DIR"`
```

This is the easiest version of your “option 4” idea, and it does **not** modify the repo.

---

### 2. Global extension reading env vars
This is the cleanest if you want pi to use env vars automatically.

Put this in:

```text
~/.pi/agent/extensions/paths-from-env.ts
```

Example:

```ts
import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent";

export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  pi.on("before_agent_start", async (event, ctx) => {
    if (!ctx.cwd.startsWith("/home/mbolshov/front_work")) {
      return;
    }

    const pairs: Array<[string, string | undefined]> = [
      ["Language spec", process.env.PI_LANG_SPEC],
      ["Test dir", process.env.PI_TEST_DIR],
      ["Build dir", process.env.PI_BUILD_DIR],
    ];

    const lines = pairs
      .filter(([, v]) => !!v)
      .map(([k, v]) => `- ${k}: \`${v}\``)
      .join("\n");

    if (!lines) {
      return;
    }

    return {
      systemPrompt:
        event.systemPrompt +
        `

## Personal canonical paths

${lines}

Prefer these paths when relevant.
`,
    };
  });
}
```

Then start pi via a wrapper:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
export PI_LANG_SPEC="docs/spec/current"
export PI_TEST_DIR="ets2panda/test"
export PI_BUILD_DIR="out/current"
exec pi "$@"
```

This avoids any repo changes.

---

### 3. Personal untracked `.pi/` inside the repo
If you want config to be per-repo but not shared, you can use `.pi/` and keep it uncommitted.

For example:

```text
.pi/extensions/paths-from-env.ts
.pi/prompts/paths.md
```

Then exclude it locally:

```bash
echo .pi/ >> .git/info/exclude
```

So:
- pi sees it
- git ignores it
- teammates don’t get it

This is a nice middle ground.

---

## What I’d recommend for your case

If the project `AGENTS.md` is shared, I’d use:

### Best setup
- a **wrapper script** that exports your path vars
- a **global extension** in `~/.pi/agent/extensions/`

That gives you:
- no repo modifications
- automatic path injection
- per-project behavior via `ctx.cwd`

---

## Important limitation of env vars
If you change env vars in your shell **after pi is already running**, pi usually won’t see that change.

So with env-based setup:
- change vars
- **restart pi**

If you want updates without restart, use a file instead, e.g.:
- `~/.config/pi/project-paths.json`
- and have the extension read that file on every prompt

That’s often better than env vars for frequently changing paths.

---

## Short answer
Yes, you can avoid touching shared `AGENTS.md` by using:

- `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md`
- `~/.pi/agent/extensions/...`
- or untracked `.pi/...` files excluded via `.git/info/exclude`

For your env-var idea, **global extension + wrapper script** is probably the best solution.

If you want, I can write a ready-to-use:
1. `run-pi.sh`
2. `~/.pi/agent/extensions/paths-from-env.ts`
3. or a file-based version with `~/.config/pi/project-paths.json`.
what is "before_agent_start" event? how more often then for example every new session start?

content.text:
pi starts
  │
  ├─► session_start { reason: "startup" }
  └─► resources_discover { reason: "startup" }
      │
      ▼
user sends prompt ─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                        │
  ├─► (extension commands checked first, bypass if found)  │
  ├─► input (can intercept, transform, or handle)          │
  ├─► (skill/template expansion if not handled)            │
  ├─► before_agent_start (can inject message, modify system prompt)
  ├─► agent_start                                          │
  ├─► message_start / message_update / message_end         │
  │                                                        │
  │   ┌─── turn (repeats while LLM calls tools) ───┐       │
  │   │                                            │       │
  │   ├─► turn_start                               │       │
  │   ├─► context (can modify messages)            │       │
  │   ├─► before_provider_request (can inspect or replace payload)
  │   ├─► after_provider_response (status + headers, before stream consume)
  │   │                                            │       │
  │   │   LLM responds, may call tools:            │       │
  │   │     ├─► tool_execution_start               │       │
  │   │     ├─► tool_call (can block)              │       │
  │   │     ├─► tool_execution_update              │       │
  │   │     ├─► tool_result (can modify)           │       │
  │   │     └─► tool_execution_end                 │       │
  │   │                                            │       │
  │   └─► turn_end                                 │       │
  │                                                        │
  └─► agent_end                                            │
                                                           │
user sends another prompt ◄────────────────────────────────┘

/new (new session) or /resume (switch session)
  ├─► session_before_switch (can cancel)
  ├─► session_shutdown
  ├─► session_start { reason: "new" | "resume", previousSessionFile? }
  └─► resources_discover { reason: "startup" }

content.text:
`before_agent_start` is a **per-prompt** extension event.

## What it means

It fires:

- **after** you submit a prompt
- **before** pi starts the agent loop for that prompt

According to pi docs, it can:

- **inject a message**
- **modify the system prompt**

So it is the right place for things like:

- adding dynamic context
- reading env/file-based config
- appending “current canonical paths”
- changing instructions for this specific request

---

## How often it fires

### `before_agent_start`
**Once per user prompt**

If in one session you ask:

1. “find failing tests”
2. “open the spec”
3. “fix this parser bug”

then `before_agent_start` fires **3 times**.

---

### `session_start`
**Once when a session is started/reloaded/switched/forked**

Docs say it fires when session is:

- `"startup"`
- `"reload"`
- `"new"`
- `"resume"`
- `"fork"`

So in a normal session where you keep chatting, `session_start` usually fires **once**, while `before_agent_start` fires **for every prompt**.

---

## Comparison

### Example: one long session with 20 prompts

- `session_start`: usually **1**
- `before_agent_start`: **20**

### Example: `/reload` then continue
- old extension runtime shuts down
- new `session_start` fires again
- then `before_agent_start` still fires on each new prompt

---

## Important distinction from turns

Inside one prompt, pi may do multiple internal **turns** if the model uses tools.

So frequency is roughly:

- `session_start` → rare
- `before_agent_start` → once per prompt
- `turn_start` → one or more times per prompt

If a single prompt causes:
- model response
- tool call
- another model response
- another tool call

then:
- `before_agent_start` still fires only **once**
- `turn_start` / `turn_end` may fire **multiple times**

---

## Practical advice for your env-path case

### Use `session_start` if:
- values are stable for the whole session
- you want to read/cache them once

### Use `before_agent_start` if:
- you want those values added to model context **on every prompt**
- or values may change between prompts and you want to re-read them

A common pattern is:

- `session_start` → read/cache config
- `before_agent_start` → append cached info to the system prompt

But if your values really can change during the session, read from a file in `before_agent_start`.

---

## Short answer

`before_agent_start` is **much more frequent** than `session_start`.

- `session_start` = when session begins/changes
- `before_agent_start` = every time you send a prompt

If you want, I can show a tiny extension example demonstrating both events side by side.
1) change to session_start 2) replace /home/mbolshov with $HOME/workspace directory and write extension into global pi configuration

content.text:
`false` in print mode (`-p`) and JSON mode. `true` in interactive and RPC mode. In RPC mode, dialog methods (`select`, `confirm`, `input`, `editor`) work via the extension UI sub-protocol, and fire-and-forget methods (`notify`, `setStatus`, `setWidget`, `setTitle`, `setEditorText`) emit requests to the client. Some TUI-specific methods are no-ops or return defaults (see [rpc.md](rpc.md#extension-ui-protocol)).

### ctx.cwd

Current working directory.

### ctx.sessionManager

Read-only access to session state. See [session.md](session.md) for the full SessionManager API and entry types.

For `tool_call`, this state is synchronized through the current assistant message before handlers run. In parallel tool execution mode it is still not guaranteed to include sibling tool results from the same assistant message.

```typescript
ctx.sessionManager.getEntries()       // All entries
ctx.sessionManager.getBranch()        // Current branch
ctx.sessionManager.getLeafId()        // Current leaf entry ID
```

### ctx.modelRegistry / ctx.model

Access to models and API keys.

### ctx.signal

The current agent abort signal, or `undefined` when no agent turn is active.

Use this for abort-aware nested work started by extension handlers, for example:
- `fetch(..., { signal: ctx.signal })`
- model calls that accept `signal`
- file or process helpers that accept `AbortSignal`

`ctx.signal` is typically defined during active turn events such as `tool_call`, `tool_result`, `message_update`, and `turn_end`.

content.thinking:
**Simplifying extension writing**

I’m thinking maybe the extension can read all environment variables matching `PI_PATH_*` or `PI_RESOURCE_*`, but the user’s request is just about changing events and paths. Since the user asked to write an extension, I should consider making it generic and mentioning the variable names. I need to check the global settings for existing extensions but could just write a new file. It seems no modification of settings is necessary since auto-discovery in `/.pi/agent/extensions` will work, so I’ll create a file under the global extension path and name it `resource-paths.ts`.

**Designing extension requirements**

I'm considering whether to read existing global settings, but it might not be necessary if auto-discovery works by placement. Instead, I can just write a file, which means I need to use `write` for creating a new file. This could be in `/.pi/agent/extensions/paths-from-env.ts` or something similar. It’s important to check if the user prefers the `$HOME/workspace` directory rather than a specific repo, ensuring the extension is active for all projects under the workspace. I'll also work on supporting session start and message insertion. The requirements include determining the workspace root, checking contexts, gathering environment variables, and avoiding duplicates.

**Choosing message display options**

I’m thinking that switching to session_start means I can send a custom message into the session context just once. This message could state, "Canonical project paths for this session: ...", and it would persist. I’m considering whether to display it as true or false. If true, the user sees it at session start, but false keeps the UI clean. Displaying it as false could mean it’s less noisy, but then the user won’t know it happened. Perhaps I should combine display as false with a notification like, "Injected N path vars into session context."

**Detecting environment variables**

I’m pondering whether having a custom message in the session be invisible is okay. I think it is, but it might complicate detecting duplicates. I need to detect environment variables generically, and I have a couple of options. One is a fixed list like PI_LANG_SPEC, PI_TEST_DIR, and PI_BUILD_DIR. The other option is a prefix-based list for variables that start with PI_PATH_ or PI_RESOURCE_. To keep things simple, I might stick with a fixed list while also supporting both fixed known vars and arbitrary variables with prefixes. That seems like the best approach.

extension writing**

I’m thinking maybe the extension can read all environment variables matching `PI_PATH_*` or `PI_RESOURCE_*`, but the user’s request is just about changing events and paths. Since the user asked to write an extension, I should consider making it generic and mentioning the variable names. I need to check the global settings for existing extensions but could just write a new file. It seems no modification of settings is necessary since auto-discovery in `/.pi/agent/extensions` will work, so I’ll create a file under the global extension path and name it `resource-paths.ts`."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Designing extension requirements**

I'm considering whether to read existing global settings, but it might not be necessary if auto-discovery works by placement. Instead, I can just write a file, which means I need to use `write` for creating a new file. This could be in `/.pi/agent/extensions/paths-from-env.ts` or something similar. It’s important to check if the user prefers the `$HOME/workspace` directory rather than a specific repo, ensuring the extension is active for all projects under the workspace. I'll also work on supporting session start and message insertion. The requirements include determining the workspace root, checking contexts, gathering environment variables, and avoiding duplicates."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Choosing message display options**

I’m thinking that switching to session_start means I can send a custom message into the session context just once. This message could state, "Canonical project paths for this session: ...", and it would persist. I’m considering whether to display it as true or false. If true, the user sees it at session start, but false keeps the UI clean. Displaying it as false could mean it’s less noisy, but then the user won’t know it happened. Perhaps I should combine display as false with a notification like, "Injected N path vars into session context.""},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Detecting environment variables**

I’m pondering whether having a custom message in the session be invisible is okay. I think it is, but it might complicate detecting duplicates. I need to detect environment variables generically, and I have a couple of options. One is a fixed list like PI_LANG_SPEC, PI_TEST_DIR, and PI_BUILD_DIR. The other option is a prefix-based list for variables that start with PI_PATH_ or PI_RESOURCE_. To keep things simple, I might stick with a fixed list while also supporting both fixed known vars and arbitrary variables with prefixes. That seems like the best approach."}]}

content.text:
/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/lib/node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/docs/extensions.md:834:ctx.sessionManager.getBranch()        // Current branch
/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/lib/node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/docs/extensions.md:1514:    for (const entry of ctx.sessionManager.getBranch()) {

content.thinking:
**Considering session management**

I'm thinking about using `getBranch`, which could be a good choice. For a new session, the custom message will only appear on the current branch. When reloading or resuming, I can check for duplicates. I might need to record an environment snapshot to see if values change. Since `session_start` occurs only once, I wonder if I should notify users if existing messages differ upon reload, but that could get confusing. Overall, I should probably avoid dynamic changes.

**Evaluating custom message behavior**

I'm finding a potential issue with the custom message being sent on `session_start` without a user prompt. I believe it does persist in the session from the first prompt, but I'm not entirely sure. If `sendMessage` is called during `session_start`, it might append the custom message immediately. I think using `display: true` is necessary to ensure the user is aware of it, although it could clutter the chat. I want to strike a balance between transparency and simplicity. I'll go with `display: true` to show the loaded paths on startup.

**Considering message visibility**

I'm pondering whether the TUI automatically shows a custom message without user prompts. It seems likely that it does. I could use `ctx.ui.notify` to give an indication. Should I set the display to true or false? If true, the message will show as a conversation entry, which is great for debugging. However, displaying it could clutter the chat. Since the user wants to simplify context, I might opt for `display: true` for visibility, but perhaps it’s more elegant to display false and notify users about the number of path variables.

**Considering directory structure**

I'm thinking about the current working directory being /home/mbolshov/front_work. Since it's not under /home/mbolshov/workspace, it seems the extension won't run for this project. The user wants to replace /home/mbolshov with the $HOME/workspace directory, likely for future use within the workspace. This could mean they want any repo under /workspace to be compatible. It might be good to create an extension that respects environment variable names and provides flexibility for managing different resource paths. I'll document usage and draft robust code.

**Evaluating sendMessage behavior**

I’m considering whether to check the documentation or code regarding the `sendMessage` idle behavior. It mentions injecting a custom message into the session, with the option for delivery as "nextTurn." If the agent is idle, it likely injects immediately, but to be safe, I think I’ll set it to deliver as "nextTurn." This way, it ensures the message is queued for the next prompt. However, I’m unsure if it appears as an actual session message before that prompt.

**Investigating message appending**

I think messages likely get appended immediately and will show up in later prompts. The file-trigger example uses `sendMessage` with `triggerTurn` set to true on `fs.watch`, but if there’s no `triggerTurn`, it probably just appends. I wonder if there's a method for appending a custom message entry directly to the session using `sessionManager`. The documentation mentions a session storage API that might include `appendCustomMessageEntry`, but I need to check if that's part of the SessionManager in the SDK.

session management**

I'm thinking about using `getBranch`, which could be a good choice. For a new session, the custom message will only appear on the current branch. When reloading or resuming, I can check for duplicates. I might need to record an environment snapshot to see if values change. Since `session_start` occurs only once, I wonder if I should notify users if existing messages differ upon reload, but that could get confusing. Overall, I should probably avoid dynamic changes."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Evaluating custom message behavior**

I'm finding a potential issue with the custom message being sent on `session_start` without a user prompt. I believe it does persist in the session from the first prompt, but I'm not entirely sure. If `sendMessage` is called during `session_start`, it might append the custom message immediately. I think using `display: true` is necessary to ensure the user is aware of it, although it could clutter the chat. I want to strike a balance between transparency and simplicity. I'll go with `display: true` to show the loaded paths on startup."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Considering message visibility**

I'm pondering whether the TUI automatically shows a custom message without user prompts. It seems likely that it does. I could use `ctx.ui.notify` to give an indication. Should I set the display to true or false? If true, the message will show as a conversation entry, which is great for debugging. However, displaying it could clutter the chat. Since the user wants to simplify context, I might opt for `display: true` for visibility, but perhaps it’s more elegant to display false and notify users about the number of path variables."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Considering directory structure**

I'm thinking about the current working directory being /home/mbolshov/front_work. Since it's not under /home/mbolshov/workspace, it seems the extension won't run for this project. The user wants to replace /home/mbolshov with the $HOME/workspace directory, likely for future use within the workspace. This could mean they want any repo under /workspace to be compatible. It might be good to create an extension that respects environment variable names and provides flexibility for managing different resource paths. I'll document usage and draft robust code."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Evaluating sendMessage behavior**

I’m considering whether to check the documentation or code regarding the `sendMessage` idle behavior. It mentions injecting a custom message into the session, with the option for delivery as "nextTurn." If the agent is idle, it likely injects immediately, but to be safe, I think I’ll set it to deliver as "nextTurn." This way, it ensures the message is queued for the next prompt. However, I’m unsure if it appears as an actual session message before that prompt."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Investigating message appending**

I think messages likely get appended immediately and will show up in later prompts. The file-trigger example uses `sendMessage` with `triggerTurn` set to true on `fs.watch`, but if there’s no `triggerTurn`, it probably just appends. I wonder if there's a method for appending a custom message entry directly to the session using `sessionManager`. The documentation mentions a session storage API that might include `appendCustomMessageEntry`, but I need to check if that's part of the SessionManager in the SDK."}]}

/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/lib/node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/docs/session.md:388:- `appendCustomMessageEntry(customType, content, display, details?)` - Extension message (in context)

- `SessionManager.listAll(onProgress?)` - List all sessions across all projects

### Instance Methods - Session Management
- `newSession(options?)` - Start a new session (options: `{ parentSession?: string }`)
- `setSessionFile(path)` - Switch to a different session file
- `createBranchedSession(leafId)` - Extract branch to new session file

### Instance Methods - Appending (all return entry ID)
- `appendMessage(message)` - Add message
- `appendThinkingLevelChange(level)` - Record thinking change
- `appendModelChange(provider, modelId)` - Record model change
- `appendCompaction(summary, firstKeptEntryId, tokensBefore, details?, fromHook?)` - Add compaction
- `appendCustomEntry(customType, data?)` - Extension state (not in context)
- `appendSessionInfo(name)` - Set session display name
- `appendCustomMessageEntry(customType, content, display, details?)` - Extension message (in context)
- `appendLabelChange(targetId, label)` - Set/clear label

### Instance Methods - Tree Navigation
- `getLeafId()` - Current position
- `getLeafEntry()` - Get current leaf entry
- `getEntry(id)` - Get entry by ID
- `getBranch(fromId?)` - Walk from entry to root
- `getTree()` - Get full tree structure
- `getChildren(parentId)` - Get direct children

content.text:
Done.

### Written file
`~/.pi/agent/extensions/paths-from-env.ts`

### What changed
- uses **`session_start`**
- applies only when pi is started in a directory under:
  - **`$HOME/workspace`**
- injects the path hints into the **session once**
- avoids duplicate injections on reload/resume
- reads:
  - `PI_LANG_SPEC`
  - `PI_TEST_DIR`
  - `PI_BUILD_DIR`
  - any extra vars matching `PI_PATH_*`
  - any extra vars matching `PI_RESOURCE_*`

### Important note
Your current repo is in:

`/home/mbolshov/front_work`

which is **outside** `$HOME/workspace`, so this extension will **not trigger here** unless:
- you run pi in a project under `~/workspace`, or
- I change/remove that workspace filter

### Next step
Run:

```text
/reload
```

or restart pi.

### Example env setup
```bash
export PI_LANG_SPEC="$HOME/workspace/specs/arkts.md"
export PI_TEST_DIR="$HOME/workspace/myproj/test"
export PI_BUILD_DIR="$HOME/workspace/myproj/out"
export PI_PATH_LSP="$HOME/workspace/tools/lsp"
```

### Behavior note
Because this now uses `session_start`, the env values are effectively a **session snapshot**.
If you change them later, start a **new session** to refresh cleanly.

If you want, I can also add a small `/paths-refresh` command to reinject updated values on demand.
ok, let's add /paths-refresh

**Considering message handling**

I'm thinking we could inject a steering or follow-up message, but it seems the old ones persist. Using before_agent_start or a context event might help filter out old custom messages, leaving only the latest one. We can keep track of a current message detail or timestamp since custom messages are persistent in the session. On session start, we'll inject once, then send a new one upon refresh while filtering previous messages of the same type. I need to double-check how context events can modify messages non-destructively.

**Checking context event structure**

I need to find out if the context event's message structure includes messages. The docs mention modifying messages non-destructively and refer to session.md for message types, which should include roles. 

To filter out old custom messages, I could scan from the end and keep the first matching customType I encounter, or perhaps just the last one if there are multiple. This will likely be useful overall. The user asked about adding /paths-refresh, so I might implement a command to send updated messages when idle. I should check the registerCommand docs for guidance.

content.thinking:
**Clarifying message handling**

I'm thinking about how to refresh path hints. Using "nextTurn" seems safer since it won't interrupt a busy process, and that way the next user prompt uses it. But if a command is run while I'm busy, I wonder if it could bypass that? I might opt for sending messages in a default way when idle. Also, I should notify users if hints get queued when I’m busy to avoid stale messages. Filtering context could help with that.

**Implementing message filtering**

I'm planning to set up a listener for the context events using `pi.on("context", async (event) => { ... check for the latest custom message ...})`. I need to understand the message structure first so I can manipulate them based on role and customType. I'll be filtering messages from the end to capture the latest one that matches my criteria. It's also a good idea to add comments for clarity. Additionally, I might need to adjust checks for duplicate messages during session starts.

**Refining session handling**

I’m thinking about how to manage old messages when resuming a session. If the branch contains old messages, I'll skip them, which is good! Now, I need to consider if the current session is under workspace and lacks environment hints but has stale old messages. I might want to use a paths-refresh command to notify that nothing was found. Also, I should notify users if hints get queued when I’m busy to avoid stale messages. Filtering context could help with that."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Implementing message filtering**

I'm planning to set up a listener for the context events using `pi.on("context", async (event) => { ... check for the latest custom message ...})`. I need to understand the message structure first so I can manipulate them based on role and customType. I'll be filtering messages from the end to capture the latest one that matches my criteria. It's also a good idea to add comments for clarity. Additionally, I might need to adjust checks for duplicate messages during session starts."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Refining session handling**

I’m thinking about how to manage old messages when resuming a session. If the branch contains old messages, I'll skip them, which is good! Now, I need to consider if the current session is under workspace and lacks environment hints but has stale old messages. I might want to use a paths-refresh command to notify that nothing was found. Sending a custom message like "No current path hints found; ignore previous ones" could be helpful, but the context filter ensures only the latest message is kept."}]}

content.text:
Added `/paths-refresh` to:

`~/.pi/agent/extensions/paths-from-env.ts`

### What it does
- reloads current path env vars
- injects a new path-hints message into the session
- if no vars are set, injects a **clearing** message instead
- keeps only the **latest** `paths-from-env` message in LLM context, so old refreshed values won’t conflict

### Scope
It still only works when `cwd` is under:

`$HOME/workspace`

### Next step
Run:

```text
/reload
```

Then use:

```text
/paths-refresh
```

### Notes
- if pi is busy, refresh is queued for the **next prompt**
- if pi is idle, refresh applies immediately to future prompts

If you want, I can also add a `/paths-show` command to print the currently loaded values without refreshing.
ok, yes for paths-show

Added `/paths-show` to:

`~/.pi/agent/extensions/paths-from-env.ts`

### What it does
- shows the **currently loaded** path hints from the latest message injected by this extension
- does **not** reread env vars
- if hints were cleared, it tells you that
- in UI mode, it opens a selectable list
- outside UI, it falls back to a notification string

### Commands now available
- `/paths-refresh` — reread env vars and update path hints
- `/paths-show` — show what is currently loaded

### Next step
Run:

```text
/reload
```

Then test:

```text
/paths-show
```

If you want, I can also add `/paths-clear` to explicitly clear loaded path hints without touching env vars.

### Resources

These settings define where to load extensions, skills, prompts, and themes from.

Paths in `~/.pi/agent/settings.json` resolve relative to `~/.pi/agent`. Paths in `.pi/settings.json` resolve relative to `.pi`. Absolute paths and `~` are supported.

| Setting | Type | Default | Description |
|---------|------|---------|-------------|
| `packages` | array | `[]` | npm/git packages to load resources from |
| `extensions` | string[] | `[]` | Local extension file paths or directories |
| `skills` | string[] | `[]` | Local skill file paths or directories |
| `prompts` | string[] | `[]` | Local prompt template paths or directories |
| `themes` | string[] | `[]` | Local theme file paths or directories |
| `enableSkillCommands` | boolean | `true` | Register skills as `/skill:name` commands |

Arrays support glob patterns and exclusions. Use `!pattern` to exclude. Use `+path` to force-include an exact path and `-path` to force-exclude an exact path.

#### packages

String form loads all resources from a package:

```json
{
  "packages": ["pi-skills", "@org/my-extension"]
}
```

Object form filters which resources to load:

```json
{
  "packages": [
    {
      "source": "pi-skills",
      "skills": ["brave-search", "transcribe"],
      "extensions": []
    }
  ]
}

[49 more lines in file. Use offset=200 to continue.]

> pi can create extensions. Ask it to build one for your use case.

# Extensions

Extensions are TypeScript modules that extend pi's behavior. They can subscribe to lifecycle events, register custom tools callable by the LLM, add commands, and more.

> **Placement for /reload:** Put extensions in `~/.pi/agent/extensions/` (global) or `.pi/extensions/` (project-local) for auto-discovery. Use `pi -e ./path.ts` only for quick tests. Extensions in auto-discovered locations can be hot-reloaded with `/reload`.

**Key capabilities:**
- **Custom tools** - Register tools the LLM can call via `pi.registerTool()`
- **Event interception** - Block or modify tool calls, inject context, customize compaction
- **User interaction** - Prompt users via `ctx.ui` (select, confirm, input, notify)
- **Custom UI components** - Full TUI components with keyboard input via `ctx.ui.custom()` for complex interactions
- **Custom commands** - Register commands like `/mycommand` via `pi.registerCommand()`
- **Session persistence** - Store state that survives restarts via `pi.appendEntry()`
- **Custom rendering** - Control how tool calls/results and messages appear in TUI

**Example use cases:**
- Permission gates (confirm before `rm -rf`, `sudo`, etc.)
- Git checkpointing (stash at each turn, restore on branch)
- Path protection (block writes to `.env`, `node_modules/`)
- Custom compaction (summarize conversation your way)
- Conversation summaries (see `summarize.ts` example)
- Interactive tools (questions, wizards, custom dialogs)
- Stateful tools (todo lists, connection pools)
- External integrations (file watchers, webhooks, CI triggers)
- Games while you wait (see `snake.ts` example)

See [examples/extensions/](../examples/extensions/) for working implementations.

## Table of Contents

- [Quick Start](#quick-start)
- [Extension Locations](#extension-locations)
- [Available Imports](#available-imports)
- [Writing an Extension](#writing-an-extension)
  - [Extension Styles](#extension-styles)
- [Events](#events)
  - [Lifecycle Overview](#lifecycle-overview)
  - [Resource Events](#resource-events)

content.text:
- Omit a key to load all of that type.
- Use `[]` to load none of that type.
- `!pattern` excludes matches.
- `+path` force-includes an exact path.
- `-path` force-excludes an exact path.
- Filters layer on top of the manifest. They narrow down what is already allowed.

## Enable and Disable Resources

Use `pi config` to enable or disable extensions, skills, prompt templates, and themes from installed packages and local directories. Works for both global (`~/.pi/agent`) and project (`.pi/`) scopes.

## Scope and Deduplication

Packages can appear in both global and project settings. If the same package appears in both, the project entry wins. Identity is determined by:

- npm: package name
- git: repository URL without ref
- local: resolved absolute path

This way, the user has both the interactive and manual options to choose from."}]}

content.text:
Yes.

## Easiest way: `pi config`
Pi has a built-in resource toggle UI.

Run:

```bash
pi config
```

Then in:

- `User (~/.pi/agent/)`
- `Extensions`
- toggle `paths-from-env.ts`

After that, run:

```text
/reload
```

That’s the cleanest per-machine solution.

---

## Manual way: `~/.pi/agent/settings.json`
Since your extension is global at:

```text
~/.pi/agent/extensions/paths-from-env.ts
```

you can disable it in the **machine-local** settings file:

```json
{
  "extensions": [
    "-extensions/paths-from-env.ts"
  ]
}
```

Paths in `~/.pi/agent/settings.json` are resolved relative to `~/.pi/agent`, so `extensions/paths-from-env.ts` is the right relative path.

Then run:

```text
/reload
```

### Re-enable
Either:
- remove that line from `settings.json`, or
- re-enable it via `pi config`

---

## If you want one-off disable for a single run
You can disable all extension discovery:

```bash
pi --no-extensions
```

Or load only specific ones explicitly:

```bash
pi --no-extensions -e ~/.pi/agent/extensions/pr-review.ts
```

But for your case, `pi config` / `settings.json` is better.

---

## Best option for “some machines only”
Use:

```text
~/.pi/agent/settings.json
```

because it is **per-machine**, unlike project files.

---

## One more option
If you want, I can also modify `paths-from-env.ts` to honor an env switch like:

```bash
export PI_DISABLE_PATHS_FROM_ENV=1
```

That gives you a very simple machine-specific on/off switch without touching `pi config`.

ok, let's create a script that will populate env with variables. I will run it manually when I need it. I need path variables for the following: runtime repo, frontend repo, build directory, directory with generated tests, directory with Language Specification. There will be different scenarios to determine all of them based on current directory. Let's start from the case when we are in build directory. If one of parent directories is named 'builds' then we are in the build directory. tests directory is build_dir/urunner-work. Runtime repo is PANDA_SOURCE_DIR:STATIC in CMakeCache.txt in build directory. frontend repo is the one where points symlink runtime_repo/tools/es2panda . Spec directory is runtime_repo/plugins/ets/doc/spec

arguments.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

usage() {
  cat <<'EOF'
Usage:
  eval "$(pi-paths-env)"

Description:
  Detect project/resource paths from the current directory and print shell export
  commands for pi-related environment variables.

Currently supported scenario:
  - Current directory is inside a build directory if any parent directory is named
    "builds". The build directory is the deepest directory on the path starting
    from that "builds" directory down to the current working directory.

Exported variables:
  - PI_BUILD_DIR
  - PI_TEST_DIR
  - PI_LANG_SPEC
  - PI_RESOURCE_RUNTIME_REPO
  - PI_RESOURCE_FRONTEND_REPO
EOF
}

if [[ "${1-}" == "-h" || "${1-}" == "--help" ]]; then
  usage
  exit 0
fi

real_pwd=$(pwd -P)

find_build_dir_from_cwd() {
  local dir="$1"
  local candidate=""

  while [[ "$dir" != "/" ]]; do
    if [[ "$(basename "$dir")" == "builds" ]]; then
      candidate="$1"
      break
    fi
    dir=$(dirname "$dir")
  done

  if [[ -z "$candidate" ]]; then
    return 1
  fi

  printf '%s\n' "$candidate"
}

read_cmake_cache_value() {
  local cache_file="$1"
  local key="$2"

  awk -F= -v key="$key" '$1 == key { print substr($0, index($0, "=") + 1); exit }' "$cache_file"
}

build_dir=$(find_build_dir_from_cwd "$real_pwd") || {
  echo "pi-paths-env: unsupported location: no parent directory named 'builds' found for $real_pwd" >&2
  exit 1
}

cmake_cache="$build_dir/CMakeCache.txt"
if [[ ! -f "$cmake_cache" ]]; then
  echo "pi-paths-env: expected CMakeCache.txt in build directory: $cmake_cache" >&2
  exit 1
fi

runtime_repo=$(read_cmake_cache_value "$cmake_cache" "PANDA_SOURCE_DIR:STATIC")
if [[ -z "$runtime_repo" ]]; then
  echo "pi-paths-env: PANDA_SOURCE_DIR:STATIC not found in $cmake_cache" >&2
  exit 1
fi
runtime_repo=$(realpath "$runtime_repo")

frontend_link="$runtime_repo/tools/es2panda"
if [[ ! -e "$frontend_link" ]]; then
  echo "pi-paths-env: expected frontend symlink/path does not exist: $frontend_link" >&2
  exit 1
fi
frontend_repo=$(realpath "$frontend_link")

build_tests_dir="$build_dir/urunner-work"
spec_dir="$runtime_repo/plugins/ets/doc/spec"

for required_dir in "$runtime_repo" "$frontend_repo" "$spec_dir"; do
  if [[ ! -d "$required_dir" ]]; then
    echo "pi-paths-env: required directory not found: $required_dir" >&2
    exit 1
  fi
done

cat <<EOF
export PI_BUILD_DIR=$(printf '%q' "$build_dir")
export PI_TEST_DIR=$(printf '%q' "$build_tests_dir")
export PI_LANG_SPEC=$(printf '%q' "$spec_dir")
export PI_RESOURCE_RUNTIME_REPO=$(printf '%q' "$runtime_repo")
export PI_RESOURCE_FRONTEND_REPO=$(printf '%q' "$frontend_repo")
EOF

edits.newText:
find_build_dir_from_cwd() {
  local dir="$1"
  local builds_parent=""

  while [[ "$dir" != "/" ]]; do
    if [[ "$(basename "$dir")" == "builds" ]]; then
      builds_parent="$dir"
      break
    fi
    dir=$(dirname "$dir")
  done

  if [[ -z "$builds_parent" ]]; then
    return 1
  fi

  dir="$1"
  while :; do
    if [[ -f "$dir/CMakeCache.txt" ]]; then
      printf '%s\n' "$dir"
      return 0
    fi

    if [[ "$dir" == "$builds_parent" ]]; then
      break
    fi

    dir=$(dirname "$dir")
  done

  echo "pi-paths-env: found parent directory named 'builds', but no CMakeCache.txt between $1 and $builds_parent" >&2
  return 1
}

content.text:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

usage() {
  cat <<'EOF'
Usage:
  eval "$(pi-paths-env)"

Description:
  Detect project/resource paths from the current directory and print shell export
  commands for pi-related environment variables.

Currently supported scenario:
  - Current directory is inside a build tree if any parent directory is named
    "builds". The build directory is detected as the nearest ancestor containing
    CMakeCache.txt between the current directory and that "builds" parent.

Exported variables:
  - PI_BUILD_DIR
  - PI_TEST_DIR
  - PI_LANG_SPEC
  - PI_RESOURCE_RUNTIME_REPO
  - PI_RESOURCE_FRONTEND_REPO
EOF
}

if [[ "${1-}" == "-h" || "${1-}" == "--help" ]]; then
  usage
  exit 0
fi

real_pwd=$(pwd -P)

find_build_dir_from_cwd() {
  local dir="$1"
  local builds_parent=""

  while [[ "$dir" != "/" ]]; do
    if [[ "$(basename "$dir")" == "builds" ]]; then
      builds_parent="$dir"
      break
    fi
    dir=$(dirname "$dir")
  done

  if [[ -z "$builds_parent" ]]; then
    return 1
  fi

  dir="$1"
  while :; do
    if [[ -f "$dir/CMakeCache.txt" ]]; then
      printf '%s\n' "$dir"
      return 0
    fi

    if [[ "$dir" == "$builds_parent" ]]; then
      break
    fi

    dir=$(dirname "$dir")
  done

  echo "pi-paths-env: found parent directory named 'builds', but no CMakeCache.txt between $1 and $builds_parent" >&2
  return 1
}

read_cmake_cache_value() {
  local cache_file="$1"
  local key="$2"

  awk -F= -v key="$key" '$1 == key { print substr($0, index($0, "=") + 1); exit }' "$cache_file"
}

build_dir=$(find_build_dir_from_cwd "$real_pwd") || {
  echo "pi-paths-env: unsupported location: no parent directory named 'builds' found for $real_pwd" >&2
  exit 1
}

cmake_cache="$build_dir/CMakeCache.txt"
if [[ ! -f "$cmake_cache" ]]; then
  echo "pi-paths-env: expected CMakeCache.txt in build directory: $cmake_cache" >&2
  exit 1
fi

runtime_repo=$(read_cmake_cache_value "$cmake_cache" "PANDA_SOURCE_DIR:STATIC")
if [[ -z "$runtime_repo" ]]; then
  echo "pi-paths-env: PANDA_SOURCE_DIR:STATIC not found in $cmake_cache" >&2
  exit 1
fi
runtime_repo=$(realpath "$runtime_repo")

frontend_link="$runtime_repo/tools/es2panda"
if [[ ! -e "$frontend_link" ]]; then
  echo "pi-paths-env: expected frontend symlink/path does not exist: $frontend_link" >&2
  exit 1
fi
frontend_repo=$(realpath "$frontend_link")

build_tests_dir="$build_dir/urunner-work"
spec_dir="$runtime_repo/plugins/ets/doc/spec"

for required_dir in "$runtime_repo" "$frontend_repo" "$spec_dir"; do
  if [[ ! -d "$required_dir" ]]; then
    echo "pi-paths-env: required directory not found: $required_dir" >&2
    exit 1
  fi
done

cat <<EOF
export PI_BUILD_DIR=$(printf '%q' "$build_dir")
export PI_TEST_DIR=$(printf '%q' "$build_tests_dir")
export PI_LANG_SPEC=$(printf '%q' "$spec_dir")
export PI_RESOURCE_RUNTIME_REPO=$(printf '%q' "$runtime_repo")
export PI_RESOURCE_FRONTEND_REPO=$(printf '%q' "$frontend_repo")
EOF

edits.newText:
find_runtime_repo_from_cwd() {
  local dir="$1"

  while [[ "$dir" != "/" ]]; do
    if [[ "$(basename "$dir")" == "static_core" ]]; then
      printf '%s\n' "$dir"
      return 0
    fi
    dir=$(dirname "$dir")
  done

  return 1
}

find_latest_build_dir() {
  local builds_dir="$1"

  if [[ ! -d "$builds_dir" ]]; then
    echo "pi-paths-env: builds directory not found: $builds_dir" >&2
    return 1
  fi

  local latest_dir
  latest_dir=$(find "$builds_dir" -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -type d -printf '%T@\t%p\n' | sort -nr | awk 'NR == 1 { print $2 }')

  if [[ -z "$latest_dir" ]]; then
    echo "pi-paths-env: no build directories found in $builds_dir" >&2
    return 1
  fi

  printf '%s\n' "$latest_dir"
}

read_cmake_cache_value() {
  local cache_file="$1"
  local key="$2"

  awk -F= -v key="$key" '$1 == key { print substr($0, index($0, "=") + 1); exit }' "$cache_file"
}

resolve_paths() {
  local cwd="$1"
  local build_dir=""
  local runtime_repo=""

  if build_dir=$(find_build_dir_from_cwd "$cwd"); then
    local cmake_cache="$build_dir/CMakeCache.txt"
    if [[ ! -f "$cmake_cache" ]]; then
      echo "pi-paths-env: expected CMakeCache.txt in build directory: $cmake_cache" >&2
      return 1
    fi

    runtime_repo=$(read_cmake_cache_value "$cmake_cache" "PANDA_SOURCE_DIR:STATIC")
    if [[ -z "$runtime_repo" ]]; then
      echo "pi-paths-env: PANDA_SOURCE_DIR:STATIC not found in $cmake_cache" >&2
      return 1
    fi
    runtime_repo=$(realpath "$runtime_repo")
  elif runtime_repo=$(find_runtime_repo_from_cwd "$cwd"); then
    runtime_repo=$(realpath "$runtime_repo")
    build_dir=$(find_latest_build_dir "$(dirname "$runtime_repo")/builds")
  else
    echo "pi-paths-env: unsupported location: could not detect build tree or runtime repo for $cwd" >&2
    return 1
  fi

  local frontend_link="$runtime_repo/tools/es2panda"
  if [[ ! -e "$frontend_link" ]]; then
    echo "pi-paths-env: expected frontend symlink/path does not exist: $frontend_link" >&2
    return 1
  fi

  local frontend_repo
  frontend_repo=$(realpath "$frontend_link")

  local build_tests_dir="$build_dir/urunner-work"
  local spec_dir="$runtime_repo/plugins/ets/doc/spec"

  for required_dir in "$runtime_repo" "$frontend_repo" "$spec_dir" "$build_dir"; do
    if [[ ! -d "$required_dir" ]]; then
      echo "pi-paths-env: required directory not found: $required_dir" >&2
      return 1
    fi
  done

  cat <<EOF
export PI_BUILD_DIR=$(printf '%q' "$build_dir")
export PI_TEST_DIR=$(printf '%q' "$build_tests_dir")
export PI_LANG_SPEC=$(printf '%q' "$spec_dir")
export PI_RESOURCE_RUNTIME_REPO=$(printf '%q' "$runtime_repo")
export PI_RESOURCE_FRONTEND_REPO=$(printf '%q' "$frontend_repo")
EOF
}

resolve_paths "$real_pwd"

content.text:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

usage() {
  cat <<'EOF'
Usage:
  eval "$(pi-paths-env)"

Description:
  Detect project/resource paths from the current directory and print shell export
  commands for pi-related environment variables.

Currently supported scenarios:
  - Current directory is inside a build tree if any parent directory is named
    "builds". The build directory is detected as the nearest ancestor containing
    CMakeCache.txt between the current directory and that "builds" parent.
  - Current directory is inside the runtime repo if the directory itself or any
    parent directory is named "static_core". The build directory is detected as
    the most recently updated subdirectory of ../builds relative to the runtime
    repo root.

Exported variables:
  - PI_BUILD_DIR
  - PI_TEST_DIR
  - PI_LANG_SPEC
  - PI_RESOURCE_RUNTIME_REPO
  - PI_RESOURCE_FRONTEND_REPO
EOF
}

if [[ "${1-}" == "-h" || "${1-}" == "--help" ]]; then
  usage
  exit 0
fi

real_pwd=$(pwd -P)

find_build_dir_from_cwd() {
  local dir="$1"
  local builds_parent=""

  while [[ "$dir" != "/" ]]; do
    if [[ "$(basename "$dir")" == "builds" ]]; then
      builds_parent="$dir"
      break
    fi
    dir=$(dirname "$dir")
  done

  if [[ -z "$builds_parent" ]]; then
    return 1
  fi

  dir="$1"
  while :; do
    if [[ -f "$dir/CMakeCache.txt" ]]; then
      printf '%s\n' "$dir"
      return 0
    fi

    if [[ "$dir" == "$builds_parent" ]]; then
      break
    fi

    dir=$(dirname "$dir")
  done

  echo "pi-paths-env: found parent directory named 'builds', but no CMakeCache.txt between $1 and $builds_parent" >&2
  return 1
}

find_runtime_repo_from_cwd() {
  local dir="$1"

  while [[ "$dir" != "/" ]]; do
    if [[ "$(basename "$dir")" == "static_core" ]]; then
      printf '%s\n' "$dir"
      return 0
    fi
    dir=$(dirname "$dir")
  done

  return 1
}

find_latest_build_dir() {
  local builds_dir="$1"

  if [[ ! -d "$builds_dir" ]]; then
    echo "pi-paths-env: builds directory not found: $builds_dir" >&2
    return 1
  fi

  local latest_dir
  latest_dir=$(find "$builds_dir" -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -type d -printf '%T@\t%p\n' | sort -nr | awk 'NR == 1 { print $2 }')

  if [[ -z "$latest_dir" ]]; then
    echo "pi-paths-env: no build directories found in $builds_dir" >&2
    return 1
  fi

  printf '%s\n' "$latest_dir"
}

read_cmake_cache_value() {
  local cache_file="$1"
  local key="$2"

  awk -F= -v key="$key" '$1 == key { print substr($0, index($0, "=") + 1); exit }' "$cache_file"
}

resolve_paths() {
  local cwd="$1"
  local build_dir=""
  local runtime_repo=""

  if build_dir=$(find_build_dir_from_cwd "$cwd"); then
    local cmake_cache="$build_dir/CMakeCache.txt"
    if [[ ! -f "$cmake_cache" ]]; then
      echo "pi-paths-env: expected CMakeCache.txt in build directory: $cmake_cache" >&2
      return 1
    fi

    runtime_repo=$(read_cmake_cache_value "$cmake_cache" "PANDA_SOURCE_DIR:STATIC")
    if [[ -z "$runtime_repo" ]]; then
      echo "pi-paths-env: PANDA_SOURCE_DIR:STATIC not found in $cmake_cache" >&2
      return 1
    fi
    runtime_repo=$(realpath "$runtime_repo")
  elif runtime_repo=$(find_runtime_repo_from_cwd "$cwd"); then
    runtime_repo=$(realpath "$runtime_repo")
    build_dir=$(find_latest_build_dir "$(dirname "$runtime_repo")/builds")
  else
    echo "pi-paths-env: unsupported location: could not detect build tree or runtime repo for $cwd" >&2
    return 1
  fi

  local frontend_link="$runtime_repo/tools/es2panda"
  if [[ ! -e "$frontend_link" ]]; then
    echo "pi-paths-env: expected frontend symlink/path does not exist: $frontend_link" >&2
    return 1
  fi

  local frontend_repo
  frontend_repo=$(realpath "$frontend_link")

  local build_tests_dir="$build_dir/urunner-work"
  local spec_dir="$runtime_repo/plugins/ets/doc/spec"

  for required_dir in "$runtime_repo" "$frontend_repo" "$spec_dir" "$build_dir"; do
    if [[ ! -d "$required_dir" ]]; then
      echo "pi-paths-env: required directory not found: $required_dir" >&2
      return 1
    fi
  done

  cat <<EOF
export PI_BUILD_DIR=$(printf '%q' "$build_dir")
export PI_TEST_DIR=$(printf '%q' "$build_tests_dir")
export PI_LANG_SPEC=$(printf '%q' "$spec_dir")
export PI_RESOURCE_RUNTIME_REPO=$(printf '%q' "$runtime_repo")
export PI_RESOURCE_FRONTEND_REPO=$(printf '%q' "$frontend_repo")
EOF
}

resolve_paths "$real_pwd"

the script now treats it as the **runtime repo** and resolves:

- **build dir** = most recently updated subdirectory of `../builds`
- **tests dir** = `$PI_BUILD_DIR/urunner-work`

```
